meta.page_title: Free Teen Karate Class — Ages 12–17 · Teens-Only, Real Training
meta.meta_description: A free intro karate class for teens 12–17 — teens-only, no children in the room. Real technique, real conditioning, real rank he has to earn. No experience needed.

hero.headline: One free class. No little kids. [[Real training.]]
hero.subheadline: A free intro class for teens 12–17. Teens-only — no children in the room. He trains with people his own age from day one.

problem.headline: You're not worried he's a bad kid. You're worried he's drifting.
problem.body: He didn't ask for karate. The worry came from somewhere else — and if you're watching one of these, you already know which one.
checklist: "Screens have swallowed him — gaming till 1am, no outlet, no friends he sees in person."
"He shrank at the middle-school jump. Slouching, mumbling, won't try anything new."
"He quits everything. Two weeks of guitar, one season of soccer, a drawer of dropped hobbies."
"He's walking home alone now. I want him able to handle himself."
"He's not in trouble — he's just drifting. No structure, nothing he's working toward."
problem.closing: Every activity you've suggested got an eye-roll. This one he'll actually want — because it's nothing like the karate he's picturing.

reframe.headline: This isn't little-kid karate.
reframe.body: Whatever you're picturing — forget it. This is a teens-only room. No 6-year-olds. No participation trophies. Here's the actual trade:

swaps.to: Real technique, drilled until it's yours

swaps.from: Standing in lines waiting your turn
swaps.to: Real conditioning — you'll actually get strong
Real sparring, when you're ready and geared up

swaps.from: Belts handed out on a schedule
swaps.to: Real rank you have to earn — nobody can hand it to you

reframe.reassurance: And you're not going to be the oldest beginner in the room. Half our teen class started at 13 or later. Everyone in there was new once — recently.

how_it_works.eyebrow: His first class, start to finish
how_it_works.headline: No spotlight. No ceremony. Just train.

image.alt: A line of teenage students in gis and gym clothes throwing punches in unison in a sunlit dojo while a black-belt instructor watches

steps.title: Book the free class
steps.body: Pick a teens-only session — a night when the room is nothing but people his age.

steps.title: Show up in gym clothes
steps.body: No gi, no bowing ceremony, no getting singled out. He joins the warm-up like everyone else.

steps.title: Train the full class, then decide
steps.body: He does the real thing end to end. No pressure, no sales pitch in front of him.

benefits.eyebrow: Two people have to say yes
benefits.headline: One class. What each of you actually gets.

columns.title: What you see
items: An hour a night off the screen — a real, physical outlet.
Confidence you can see: in his posture, his voice, how he carries himself.
Structure and a goal ladder — belts he works toward, week over week.
A coach in his corner who isn't you.

columns.title: What he gets
items: Get strong — real conditioning, not warm-up games.
Train with people your own age, not a room of little kids.
Earn rank nobody can hand you.

risk_reversal.headline: If he walks out and says it's not for him, that's a complete answer.
risk_reversal.body: The free class costs you nothing and commits you to nothing. No contract, month-to-month after. If it's not for him, you'll know — and you'll never get a sales call. And if he asks when he can go back? That's the only sign you need.

items.a: No — there's a dedicated beginner-friendly teens class, and late starters are the norm, not the exception. Most of the room started as teenagers. Nobody expects him to know anything on day one.

items.q: What if he tries it and quits like everything else?
items.a: Then you've spent nothing to find out. The class is free, and after that it's month-to-month — no contract trap. But teens who stick past the first month tend to stay, because for once they're not being dragged; they're choosing it.

items.comment: TODO: confirm the school's real sparring policy + required gear.
items.a: TODO: confirm with the school. (Common honest answer: sparring is earned, supervised, opt-in, and gear is required — beginners drill technique long before they ever spar.)

items.q: Does he need to be in shape first?
items.a: No — getting in shape is what the class builds. Conditioning is part of the training, not a prerequisite for it. He starts where he is.

items.comment: Only teen→adult mention allowed (non-goal §4: no adult upsell).
items.a: At 16+ he can train alongside the adult program, and there's a natural path into it at 18 — so the progress he builds now doesn't hit a wall.
